comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
At this link is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts. Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below and/or in the comments at the current prayer list post.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.


May David/Trubrujah's 5 year old nephew Jayce, who is starting chemotherapy for leukemia on Monday 12/9, be healed quickly and fully, and may he, and mother Amanda, and their family find be aided with physical, mental, and emotional strength while they deal with this new life altering situation.

May baby Gigi, continue to gain weight and strength, and continue to heal from a possible medication overdose which her mother Elena received during pregnancy, and may Elena be blessed and healed from the continuing random tremors which ensued; may Gigi's big brother Francis continue to be in excellent health and be blessed.

May Jennifer, whose pregnancy has entered its third trimester, have a safe and healthy pregnancy, may the delivery go smoothly, and may her baby be born healthy and blessed.

May Quin's father Bob in Austin, whose aortic stent procedure went smoothly, heal quickly from the procedure, and may his health improve over the long term as a result.

May Charlie the cat, who has been extremely sick and vomiting but whose owners can't afford an expensive vet visit, be blessed, protected, and healed.

May Scotlyn's friend Fiona, who has been in hospital since early October with what is a diagnosis of ovarian cancer, be blessed and healed, and encouraged in ways that help her to maintain a positive mental and spiritual outlook.

May Annette have a successful resolution for her kidney stones, and a safe and easy surgery to remove the big one blocking her left kidney.

May Peter Evans in California, who has been diagnosed with colon cancer, be completely healed with ease, and make a rapid and total recovery.

May Jennifer and Josiah, their daughter Joanna, and their unborn daughter be protected from all harmful and malicious influences, and may any connection to malign entities or hostile thought forms or projections be broken and their influence banished.

May Ram, who is facing major challenges both legal and emotional with a divorce and child custody dispute, be blessed with the clarity of thought, positive energy, and the inner strength to continue to improve the situation.

May FJay peacefully birth a healthy baby at home with her loved ones. May her postpartum period be restful and full of love and support. May her older child feel surrounded by her love as he adapts to life as a big brother and may her marriage be strengthened during this time.

May all living things who have suffered as a consequence of Hurricanes Helene and Milton be blessed, comforted, and healed.

May Giulia (Julia) in the Eastern suburbs of Cleveland Ohio be healed of recurring seizures and paralysis of her left side and other neurological problems associated with a cyst on the right side of her brain and with surgery to treat it.

May Corey Benton, whose throat tumor has grown around an artery and won't be treated surgically, be healed of throat cancer.

May Kyle's friend Amanda, who though in her early thirties is undergoing various difficult treatments for brain cancer, make a full recovery; and may her body and spirit heal with grace.

Lp9's hometown, East Palestine, Ohio, for the safety and welfare of their people, animals and all living beings in and around East Palestine, and to improve the natural environment there to the benefit of all.


* * *

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. If this appeals to you, I invite you to join me.

comments.title: Thoughts on Devotional Polytheism, and Question on Incorporating the Shadow
comments.content:
Good Evening,

As we are coming to the end of the year, I hope that you are wrapping up what you need to and looking forward to what the new year might bring you.

To Share: This week, I've written up a pretty short rundown of Galina Krasskova's Devotional Polytheism: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_devotional_polytheism

To Ask: Do you believe that "incorporating your Shadow" (in a Jungian sense) is a useful thing to try to do in occult work generally, as opposed to only within a more narrowly Jungian approach to things? And if so, would you have any tips on how best to go about it besides "active imagination and journaling," and anything to look out for/avoid?

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Devotional Polytheism, and Question on Incorporating the Shadow
replies.content: As I see it, you can't actually "incorporate the Shadow" because the Shadow is an archetype, thus transpersonal -- to try to incorporate it would produce one form of ego inflation, the dreary sort where somebody claims to be the evilest evil that ever eviled. What you can do instead is stop projecting the archetype of the Shadow onto other people and things, and in the process, acknowledge those elements of the Shadow that the archetype borrows from your personality -- that is, the things about yourself you don't want to admit. That's best done through meditation, journaling, and if you have the opportunity, long honest talks with someone who knows you well and will point out to you the things you aren't letting yourself notice about yourself.

comments.title: Upside Down Tarot
comments.content: Hello, Liam here - when drawing a card for the day in Tarot, would you recommend paying any attention to whether one pulls the card right or wrong way up, and why? Cheers!

replies.content: Yes. I learned to read tarot with reversals as an important factor. The book I used early on is Mastering Tarot by Eden Gray, and it gives meanings for each card, upright and reversed; it might be a useful place to start.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I've been considering joining AODA, in order to become a monk through the GCC. I've been through bad group experiences in pagan circles that messed me over for a while, that makes me hesitate, but I also think I would benefit from some training and maybe a little mentoring. If I can ask, JMG and anyone else currently involved, how is the organization holding up today? Is it still going strong, keeping the political rot at bay, at least relatively drama free and an overall positive experience that you would recommend to others?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I haven't been active in the organization for a while now, but as far as I know it's still doing quite well. One thing to keep in mind is that it's not a Neopagan organization -- it dates from a much older era of esoteric traditions. (Yeah, yeah, we all know that Neopagan traditions date from the dawn of time, but in the US "the dawn of time" was around 1978.) The older organizations very often do without the drama and the bad behavior that make so many Neopagan groups so tiresome.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends on what you consider "political rot".

AODA recently updated their Ethics statement (https://aoda.org/aoda-membership/ethics/), specifically the second paragraph.

The GCC has added a note about "titles" (https://aoda.org/aoda-structure/gnostic-celtic-church-gcc/requirements-for-ordination/).

There are more examples, but I think this is sufficient to give you an idea. There are public parts of the forum (https://forum.aoda.org/) where you can either lurk or participate to get an idea of the types of interactions on there, so you can judge for yourself if you would be comfortable and have a positive experience there.

replies.title: (no subject)

Polychrome Wandering Wombat Flaneur

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I think the problem is this: can you be NON-accepting/inclusive of current identity categories. In other words, suppose my position is "If you are born with an XY chromosome pairing, you are a MALE, and if you are born with an XX pairing you are a FEMALE, and no amount of semantic tap-dancing or wishing really, really hard or hormones or surgery is going to change that". Is THAT a position that is "allowed". My general experience is that, when people start talking about how "accepting" or "affirming" or "inclusive" they are, that means the answer is NO, that position is not welcome, whether you espouse it or keep it secret. Just thinking that way means you are a bad person and need to be shunned. So when I see language like that, I walk.

replies.title: (no subject)

Are they relatively small in number, politely tolerated as anyone else would be but not allowed to control the conversation? Can someone who disagrees with them, either from the right or left, also speak? As far as drama, I get the impression most members are solitary. There have been a lot of new members in the past couple years; occasionally one will try to get political - usually on the Discord app - but the old guard does a good job of handling it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm not at all active in the organization but I did sign up while ago and thus receive their periodic newsletters via email.

I regret to say that their most recent yearly newsletter is infested with DEI language. Though I'm not sure if this language reflects the sincere beliefs of the people who run the organization, or if it was simply inserted as a defensive measure, i.e. from a place of fear (of being "cancelled"?); the DEI interpolations do read like they were copypasted out of some corporate HR manual.

Secondly, our host is right that officially AODA is not a Neopagan organization, at least as far as its instructional materials are concerned. But, going by various member contributions in the newsletters, it quite clear (to me at least) that the group is culturally-Neopagan, at least to some degree. It's like a Christian Church can rightfully call itself an ancient tradition, but if there is a big rainbow flag flying out front, we know right away what the pastors are actually preaching about inside.(See JMG's many remarks about entryism)

On a more positive note, I do believe there are a few senior members of the organization who are regular posters here, and they seem like very good people. So hopefully one of them can chime in and provide a more cheery perspective.

Finally, a little thing about groups today; I'm of the belief that practically none of them are worth joining, they all seem politicized to some degree. When I went looking for pagan groups that weren't infested with far-left woke rot, I only really found pagan groups there were infested with far-right racialist/identitarian rot, or something similar. It might be worth it to wait things out and let the current state of political polarization die down.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good morning and thank you as always for hosting this space.

I’ve been thinking about how many people perhaps don’t grasp that there are downsides to mindfulness training—especially the McMindfulness vipassana that is increasingly popular—one thinker on esotericism I admire argued that it’s essentially gaslighting naive westerners into depersonalizing themselves.

Conversely it does seem to me that there are meaningful advantages as well, especially in terms of being able to step back from the contents of one’s own mind and not just be ruled by it.

Are there good ways to achieve at least some of the advantages of mindfulness training without essentially dissolving the self?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The benefits you get from mindfulness meditation are the same you can get from any other kind of meditation. The problems with mindfulness meditation are unique to that system. Thus, yes, there are good ways to get the benefits without the problems: practice any other kind of meditation you like. One I particularly recommend to people in Western industrial cultures is discursive meditation, which I've discussed here and also in many of my books.

comments.title: Dolmen Arch, Second Religiosity, Changes in Consciousness
comments.content:
I had to slow down and focus for a month on the Two Dragons rite in The Dolmen Arch. After a full month of daily practice, I concluded I was ready to charge my palm centers… and (of course) I found myself beneath a week-long winter storm. Kinda hard to get to my favorite rock when it is under a deep drift. Doesn’t matter. My free time has been going to snow removal. Bleah. So – daily practice until the weather breaks because I really want to do the charging rite outside. Anyway – the text says to avoid polluted or stagnant water – check. But… the creek in my back yard is typical of Northeast Ohio in having some unpleasant history. Now, it flows freely to move spring water and street runoff while supporting some wildlife. Do you think it is acceptable for the purpose of charging my palm centers?

I can report another member of the intelligentsia embracing the second religiosity. My sister, transplant to the Left Coast from the Rust Belt, embracer of il-liberal vacuity, PhD psychology and former angry atheist, just shocked everyone including herself by following family tradition to join a prominent fraternal body AND take the office of chaplain. I suppose this is some kind of cosmic balance for me taking up Druidry – I go for the future, so she goes for the past’s future. (Sigh.) Or do I overstate?

And – this might fall under the magic of changes in consciousness: I observe here in a Red corner of Ohio a general political lightening of mood, including grumbling about looney lefty social policies morphing into satirizing them. Also, MAGA regalia on members of formerly Democratic-safe demographics is appearing occasionally and loaded with punch-up rather than punch-down attitude. Have you or the commentariat noticed either trend? Is anything to be made of them?

Many thanks!
Rhydlyd

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch, Second Religiosity, Changes in Consciousness
replies.content:
1) If the stream supports wildlife and plant life, it's probably okay. Go ahead and give it a try; if you end up feeling queasy after doing the charging, find someplace else.

2) Fraternal orders are probably another wave of the future -- I've watched several Masonic jurisdictions begin to get their act together and attract young men, for example. Your sister may be moving with the times, just in a slightly different direction.

3) I've seen some of the same thing here. I don't think it's accidental that the murder of that health-insurance CEO the other day got a bipartisan response from the masses amounting to "Okay, that's a good start." Things are in motion.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch, Second Religiosity, Changes in Consciousness
replies.content:
Thanks!
And I think moving with the times might be more accurate to describe my sister. She doesn't have the same umm... whatever it was about Kingsnorth's defection that rubbed my fur wrong. A certain fraternity and its bodies are growing again here after a harsh contraction caused some consolidation. I hope this is part of a broader trend.
Also, I didn't connect the CEO with the parody by the bottom of the heap of virtue signaling and victim group affiliation bingo, but... yeah, he's right in the bracket being skewered.
R

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch, Second Religiosity, Changes in Consciousness
replies.content: Oh, granted. For an atheist, moving into any relationship with spirit is a step up; for someone who's already (at least in theory) experienced an initiatory path, to turn around and parrot canned falsehoods about those paths because he's joined a church and wants to please his new friends -- well, that's quite another matter.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a question about the success and failure of religions: What is it what made the Neopagan movement fail, while other religious movements, old ones and new ones, were successful establishing themselves as small or large presences for a more or less long timespan? After all, it didn't begin in its current grubby condition. Might it have been a lack of a flake filter? The problem seems not to have had anything to do with belonging to a counter-culture, after all, Christians were in the Roman Empire part of a counter-culture, like modern Neopagans - but modern Neopagans by and large don't belong to the desposed classes of Western culture. Might it have been this factor? Can there be drawn any useful conclusions out of this subject, and if so, which conclusions?

Thanks again, by the way, for all your work!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a very good question to which I don't have a definite answer. I know that I became sure Neopaganism would implode when I saw Neopagan merchants selling bumper stickers saying "Back Off, I'm A Goddess" -- that made it clear that what a great many Neopagans were worshipping was themselves, and autolatry (the worship of one's own ego) is never a workable basis for a religious movement. But what inherent flaws or accidental problems sent the movement on its current trajectory down the ol' crapperoo is a subtler question.

replies.title: ebooks
replies.content:
I resolved against eBooks when I learned that the publisher could just suck a book back into the ozone without notice. There was some major book being issued back when eBooks were just coming out that turned out to be a forgery. It was a big scandal at the time but I don't recall the title. The publisher hadn't shipped physical copies to the bookstores yet so just cancelled orders and pulped the books. However, they had already transmitted copies to purchasers with eBooks and did remove them remotely. And my reaction was
WHOA! they can do that?

As it turned out I did win a Nook (Barnes and Noble eBook) in a raffle a few years later and have purchased a few books--how can you turn down the complete works of Trollope for only $.99?--but I don't rely on it for anything really important to me. This entire trend of treating electronic goods such as books, and software programs as only a loan or rental rather than as a purchase is very disturbing. I want the stormtroopers to have to at least tromp into my library in their jackboots and pry my books from my cold, dead hands, not merely enter an electronic order.

Rita

replies.title: Re: ebooks
replies.content: This is why I use my ebook readers only for free books from Project Gutenberg and the like, and don't connect them directly to the internet -- I download books to my internet computer and then use a cable to put them on my readers. I have zero interest in participating in the rent-a-book gimmick.

replies.title: Re: ebooks
replies.content:
There is a program called Calibre, which is very useful for storing and organizing free ebooks on a computer. And from your laptop or desktop, you can use an ethernet cable to move them from Calibre onto an ebook reader. I use a Kobo ebook reader, and like it.

https://calibre-ebook.com/

La Petite

replies.title: Re: ebooks
replies.content:
A few further thoughts: with older Kindles, you can download the book and transfer it via USB cable to the Kindle, including books which have Amazon's atrocious DRM (which also drove me away from ebooks, after finding them tremendously convenient when I used to travel all the time). Some of these older kindles don't even have internet access, which further secures them from after-the-fact meddling (you can turn it off on ones that have it built in, but never trust software "off" for internet access). Some newer books, especially those with pictures, aren't compatible with this (which I learned to my dismay when I got an electronic copy of Maria Kvilhaug's The Seed of Yggdrasill so that search could compensate for the lack of an index in my print copy).

I'd also second Calibre, which is wonderful, and besides being super handy for organizing books (in a variety of formats, including .pdfs!) has some plugins available that folks might find interesting in terms of widening the kind of books you can read with the program.

All that said, I've taken a strong "get a physical copy when possible" policy and don't regret it one bit.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To #1: It's well recognized that rhyme (like many other sorts of striking verbal patterns) enhance spellwork. Elizabeth Nesbit emphasized this in her later children's novels that feature her magical creature, the Mouldiwarp, which can only be summoned by rhyming. In academic circles there is a field of study called "ethnography of communication," which provides something of a dry theoretical basis for the phenomenon; the late Dell H. Hymes contributed a lot to this field.

Gerald Gardner's "Book of Shadows" includes this short text on the subject, which seems to me to be spot on:

"Of spells, the exact words matter little if the intent be clear and you raise the true power, and sufficient thereof. Always in rhyme they are. There is something queer about rhyme. I have tried, and the same seem to lose their power if you miss the rhyme. Also in rhyme, the words seem to say themselves. You do not have to pause and think, "What comes next?" Doing this takes away much of your intent."


To #2: I don't known how well-known this phenomenon is in magical circles, but it's a real thing between mothers--not so much fathers--and their children, and also between long and happily married couples.

My take on this starts from the notion that every grown person has multiple selves which have slowly taken shape from infancy into old age. Upon birth, children begin shaping their first self, which they do in connection with their mothers, so that a child's first self is a joint self, shared with its mother. As the years go by, children develop other, independent selves, too; and these may displace their first self. By puberty, a child has also developed alternate selves, some of which the child will hide from its parents more or less successfully, while it flaunts at least one other self openly. (Every child has to learn how to keep secrets from its parents if it is to grow up fully.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I think that a stupid, hammy rhyme has more potency than well structured poetry because they get so deeply stuck in your mind. That idiot "It's a small world after all" or the even more demented "This is the Song that has no end" tune that circles endlessly in your mind, etching deeper and deeper grooves, that's the kind of thing that can carry a magical intent deeply into the unconscious and out the other side into the world to make all kinds of merry mischief. But on the other hand, I can barely remember one line of Poe's "The Raven"...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To be fair, in the examples given, it might have as much to do with being set to a tune as with rhyming (as hard as it is for modern English speakers, myself very much included, to think about tunes without rhymes). Rhythm/cadence/tone seem to have really strong effects on "stickiness" - an effect I've gotten a chance to notice in a kind of backwards way.

One of the main things I teach is public speaking, and one of the ways you can tell if someone tried to memorize their speech word-for-word, but didn't put in enough practice to fake doing it "naturally" (which, to be fair, is a whole lot of work. It's called "acting."), is that their phrases take on a repetitive cadence. My working theory is that we have a certain length of "working memory," and so when we're trying to memorize something, we divide the thing to be memorized into chunks roughly that big, then, to make them more memorable, we impose a rhythm on them, but since it's half-conscious at best, we use the same rhythm for each chunk. So you get something vaguely sing-songy.

Point being, when the rhythm is more intentionally crafted, and genuinely heard (sometimes hard to get from just reading poetry, even if it was composed metrically), I think it does a lot to make a given chunk of words brain-sticky. Combine it with rhyme, and you're cooking with gas.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: lesser  ritual of the hexagram
comments.content:
Hello.

I am wondering what the general effect of the lesser banishing ritual of the hexagram is.

Also, I am wondering why we don't often hear about the less invoking ritual of the hexagram.

Thank you,

Celadon Exuberant Ocelot

replies.title: Re: lesser  ritual of the hexagram
replies.content:
The Golden Dawn tradition is a vast attic full of interesting things, more than half of which have been more or less neglected since the original order blew itself to smithereens in political squabbles in 1903. The lesser hexagram rituals are among those neglected items. They invoke and banish the planetary energies collectively, just as the lesser pentagram rituals invoke and banish the elemental energies collectively.

In the old teachings of Renaissance magic, there are basically three levels of magical energy we all work with -- elemental, astrological, and spiritual. (Cornelius Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy assigns one book to each of these.) The influences of the spiritual realm can't be invoked or banished by ritual means -- the angels, who are its inhabitants, are far more powerful than we are. The influences of the astrological and elemental realms, however, are subject to us. Thus we use the LBRP to clear away elemental energies before invoking the force of one particular element, and to clear things away afterwards as well.

The LBRH does the same thing on the astrological level. If you want to invoke a planetary force, say, you start by clearing away the elemental static with the LBRP, and then you clear away the influence of the other planets with the LBRH; then you invoke the planet you want. As for the LIRH, neither of the lesser invoking rituals are that useful in practice, since it's very rare that you'd have any use for undifferentiated elemental or planetary force -- you want a specific force.

comments.title: Divided Will
comments.content:
JMG,

Last week's MM had a question about learning to meditate which you answered with a reference to divided will. Here is the quote from your reply:

"More generally, it's been my constant experience that people who approach spirituality that way -- with an attitude of "show me something to prove to me that it's real" -- have a divided will on the subject. They want results but they also fear them, and the closer they get to anything the stronger the fear becomes. I've seen people of this kind experience something strange and then immediately go to work rationalizing it out of existence, or even panicking and insisting that they never experienced anything at all.

The inner ambivalence has to be resolved before spiritual experience becomes possible. The spiritual world is all around us all the time. It's not that it's not there -- it's that most of us are hiding from it."

My question is - how do you resolve that "inner ambivalence"? Meditation? Journaling? Introspection? Therapy?

I don't have it in relation to spirituality but it cast a light for me on various other goals that have stalled - likely because of the phenomenon you have described.

To give one example (out of many): I am learning kickboxing. My coach tells me my theoretical understanding is very good but I need to let go of my logical/theoretical mind and just throw myself into kicking without reservation - almost like a child, without fear, going "all in". Why do I not do what he says? Well, I try to - but I have a considerable amount of subconscious fear that sort of acts as an internal brake and makes me hesitate or go slower than I am capable of because I have had previous injuries to my foot (not from kickboxing) and am really concerned about a bad injury.

The coach says hesitating/slowing down is actually MORE dangerous and MORE likely to lead to injuries because the technique was designed to be done at speed and doing it like I do, breaks the mechanics of the technique and it's not just weaker in power, it also messes with my balance and how my weight is supposed to shift and the impact is not correct and basically I'm MORE likely to get hurt.

And yet - I know this logically but I really struggle to take the brakes off and go "all in" - it sounds exactly like the "divided will" you mentioned.

(It is also possible that this is a self-image issue - if at some subconscious level I think of myself as a "fat middle-aged dad who isn't good at kickboxing", then it is possible that I am subconsciously holding myself back from making significant improvements in my kickboxing because that would break my identity of self-image of myself).

I guess it could be both issues, or neither (and they may be linked) - but the question remains how to get through to the next level?

replies.title: Re: Divided Will
replies.content: Nearly all of us have divided wills on a whole series of issues, and getting past that is one of the great challenges of human existence. Meditation, journaling, the Octagon Society exercises, counseling, massage, dozens of different kinds of therapy -- they can all play a role in resolving it. The crucial thing, though, is to realize that this is not something that is being done to you. It's something you're doing, and you can stop doing it.

comments.title: the fading of magic
comments.content:
Last week, there was an exchange where you agreed that you'd bring magic & the supernatural back into the world, to a far greater extent than there is currently. I would do the same, even if I might be opening Pandora's box/jar/pithos for all I know.

But there's a tangent here. Other times, I've brought up fictional stories, especially in video games I've played, and their surprising correspondences with occult lore (eg fairly accurate retellings of Atlantis, intelligent dinosaurids in the Mesozoic, etc).
One of those recurring themes is the loss of magic from the world. Usually this happens because of the gross abuse of magic, for tyrannical or even insane/nihilistic purposes, and defeating the evil entity responsible inevitably means also cutting off the source of magic, resulting in a saved, yet disenchanted world.

And so, I'm wondering what the occult significance might be. Is this referring to a specific sequence of events long ago, or something else?

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content:
That's a very complex issue. First of all, certain kinds of magic haven't gone anywhere; anyone who's willing to invest half an hour a day in basic occult practices will find out that they work, and can transform your life quite effectively. Equally, of course, as Ioan Couliano pointed out many years ago, advertising and propaganda follow the classic rules of magic; they're forms of debased evil magic that hold many people in their grip.

Second, most modern societies are profoundly neurotic about magic. I've discussed in a series of blog posts the way this has shaped the popular belief that magic went away:

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-mask-of-disenchantment/
https://www.ecosophia.net/the-nature-of-enchantment/
https://www.ecosophia.net/against-enchantment-i-ken-wilber/
https://www.ecosophia.net/against-enchantment-2-owen-barfield/
https://www.ecosophia.net/against-enchantment-3-jean-gebser/
https://www.ecosophia.net/the-destiny-of-disenchantment/

The basic theme of these posts, if you want to spare yourself the work of wading through them, is that this notion tht the world is disenchanted is a vast pretense, meant to justify a set of attitudes and policies that prop up our dysfunctional societies.

Third, there does seem to be some evidence, though it's equivocal, that magic had more obvious effects on the material plane in earlier eras of history. Native American philosopher Vine Deloria Jr. discussed this in his last book, The World We Used To Live In, which is well worth reading. I used the same ideas as part of the framing of my tentacle novels, for whatever that's worth. What might be behind it -- ah, that's an interesting and subtle issue, which I touched on in the sequence of posts listed above.

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content:
I appreciate your comprehensive answer!

To clarify, I certainly didn't mean to imply that magic is not with us, because it definitely is -- as I've mentioned on here, I've had way too many experiences that defy all materialism & rationalism. There are other ways to interpret the aforementioned theme -- that magic is at low tide, so to speak, or that we've become estranged from it.
I'll have to dig into those posts, and the book; thanks for the recs. If I can ask a follow-up: if it's true that magic had more noticeable material effects in the past, would that explain the miracles and supernatural occurrences in Scriptures and old tales, and the sense that such fantastic things only happened "once upon a time"?

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content: That's the basic theme of Deloria's book -- that magic works now, but it was much more powerful in the past and had more dramatic effects. I've sometimes wondered if that's one of the things that the precessional cycle governs; it may be that magic is strongest in the ages ruled by Sun and Moon (the ages of Leo and Cancer) and weakest in the ages traditionally ruled by Saturn (Aquarius and Capricorn). If that's the case, we've got close to 4200 years before magic starts picking up material strength again -- but at least we can take some comfort in the facts that this is as bad as it gets, and we've got some solid magic to carry us through until the tide starts flowing in again.

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content:
I have thought about this in the context of my research into the temple tech. It does seem that the ancient monuments constructed to those largely forgotten principles had more power once upon a time, and whilst I an convinced that this force is etheric in nature, if there's been any general 'dimming of the powers', then I think it will be related. It could be as simple as the astrological ages, being down here in the time of Aquarius, we are as far removed from the gods as the cycles get. So certain powers will be dimmed, but other powers, i.e. the powers of the material world (and potentially, the sub-natural too), will be enhanced. That may mean quite simply, our task out here on the end, is to be confronted with the realities of material nature, and transcend them individually.

But I think there is more to it - the earth's magnetic field has been declining for a long time. The magnetic force is the closest thing to an ether we have in science (and isn't fully understood, doesn't obey a bunch of symmetry conditions that other forces do, and so on). It was once much stronger too.

So yes, some kinds of magic will no longer be as strong. Other kinds, the kinds that Levi taught, may be stronger than they were.

Intriguingly, I suspect that none of the above applies to the mental planes and above, that seem to be above this waxing and waning cycle.

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content: The magnetic field is another issue, and one that could reach crisis at any point -- it seems likely to me that we could see a complete collapse of the magnetic field sometime soon, and then the usual period of confusion before a new one gets established and all our compass needles point south instead of north. That is to say, it may be a complex matter.

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content:
As for the magnetic field not obeying symmetry conditions -- I'm not a mathematical physicist, but my understanding is that there's a perspective called "Clifford algebra" or "geometric algebra" (not to be confused with "algebraic geometry") where, rather than thinking of magnetic fields as being spatial vectors, like little invisible directed arrows of various lengths floating in space, that just happen to have curious handedness conventions for their interactions with electrical currents, you instead think of magnetic fields as being bivectors -- which, in three dimensions, as far as I understand means that they are like little invisible directed cross-sectional areas of various sizes floating in space. Somehow, in this perspective, there is no need for a right-hand rule and no asymmetry/handedness/chirality issue. The geometric algebra perspective on electromagnetism lets you compress all four of Maxwell's equations into just six or so symbols, and thus by parsimony it is probably much more natural and correct.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebra_of_physical_space#Maxwell's_equations_and_the_Lorentz_force

If you want to understand electromagnetism deeply for purposes of having good intuitions about temples, or probably also for having good intuitions about ethers insofar as they are analogous to electromagnetism, it might benefit you in unexpected ways to get into the headspace where the abstract geometric entities in the geometric-algebra formulation of Maxwell's equations (mostly, that means graded vector spaces, the wedge and inner products, and the geometric product) feel entirely natural. I don't have good references to recommend for help with developing that intuition, though.

(This is in addition to my previous implied advice about how you need to develop good intuitions about waveguides and resonators. And the other implied advice about how, if you were to plan to be making any engineering judgements involving situations where energy is being changed in frequency, or actively focused rather than passively resonated with, then you would also need to develop good intuitions about basic weak forms of nonlinearity. If it helps, you can get the beginning of an intuition for weak nonlinearity effects in oscillators by looking at moiré patterns, which are what you get when you multiply together intensities of transparency that oscillate across space, and then thinking by analogy about what would happen if you were instead multiplying together intensities of field strength that oscillate across time.)

Or, by "doesn't obey a bunch of symmmetry conditions", do you mean symmetry at the level that particle physicists talk about, like charge/parity/time-reversal symmetry? I don't know a lot about that, but I thought that at that level magnetic forces were much more clearly just the opposite side of the same coin as electrical forces, and from the perspective of considerations of symmetry at the level of particle physics, I don't think electromagnetism as a whole is thought to be any stranger than any other force.

I guess I already talked at you about my two pet hypotheses about ethers, including the one about what happens when you don't arbitrarily impose the cylinder condition in the Kaluza-Klein way of hypothetically constructing electromagnetism as a geometrical phenomenon.

replies.title: Re: the fading of magic
replies.content:
Wow, I will add all of that to the list. Your first list is still in my to do section in the book, the second list will be duly added.

The issue with magnetism, unlike electricity, is that there's no monopoles, there's always a north, south direction on each charge, no matter how many times you cut them in half. It's not symmetrical with electricity, whereby charges can be separated. That and the Aharanov Bohm effect make me think that magnetism is upstream of electricity.

comments.title: monastic training and irganization
comments.content:
Since monasticism occasionally comes up, I thought I'd mention a book that I've just finished reading -- perhaps due to a recommendation here; I don't remember.

The book is Jamie Kreiner's The Wandering Mind: What Medieval Monks Tell Us About Distraction (2023), not all expensive for the value, and probably available through public libraries (which is how I obtained it).

It's a very accessible discussion of Christian (eastern, western, and other: for example, Nestorian documents found at Dunhuang) monastic traditions -- organization, training, and disciplines, largely focused on the earlier centuries, but getting up as far, at times, as about the 13th century. Despite the accessible style, it is very well documented and footnoted, so that the author's remarks and sources are easy to follow up and check into. She has coordinated a huge amount of very specialized, and widely dispersed, material into what is, if nothing else, a very handy guide to the literature(s).

However, it's much more than that, since she's interested in what monks did, and how and why they did it, and in making all that make sense to modern readers. She also makes the point that monasticism was an experimental pursuit, with very specific goals, and that methods were often developed and adjusted to fit actual circumstances. And copiously documented. Even now, extensive records survive.

She also has a certain amount of valuable comparative material (referring to other monastic traditions). Perhaps somewhere there is or could be a field of comparative monasticism, covering not only institutional forms, but also lived experience, methods of training and cultivation, and so on. She has quite a bit to say about the art of memory, levels of prayer, and so on.

People interested in working out ways to explore or pursue a monastic vocation outside the readily available varieties (Buddhist, Christian, etc.) could find this a very valuable resource. She spends a lot of time on the early (anchoritic, eremitic) period, and its growth into institutional monastic forms. She also has a great deal to say about the ways in which monks (even anchorites and hermits) interacted with and served their communities.

[In her usage, by the way, "monk" is a non-gendered term that refers both to male and female monastics (from "monachos" and "monacha", collapsed into a single English term) -- even though "nun" is generally available as a term for female monastics (as in "Buddhist nun"). So there are frequently such sentences as, "A monk would generally arrange her day according to a specific schedule". It's not an intrusive stylistic choice, but it does mean that there can be some ambiguity about whether she is talking about monks in general, or something pertaining specifically to female monastics.]

Well worth a read.

replies.title: Re: monastic training and irganization
replies.content: Hmm! Many thanks for this; I'll have a look at it, and see if it's suitable to recommend to prospective members of the Order of the Universal Monk and Order of the Universal Nun.

replies.title: Re: monastic training and irganization
replies.content:
Relating to monasticism and its discussions -- as the Archdruid said, we're not sure why celibacy works for monks, but it just does, so we need to stick with the technology that works. It's essentially a case of Chesterson's Fence. Also relevant is this article, which has a rationalist outlook and ignores possible magical properties, but is otherwise a fine diagnosis: https://scholars-stage.org/tradition-is-smarter-than-you-are/
As also stated, the Shakers had the potential to be the American monastery, and they did very well under the circumstances, but their fatal flaw was their lack of a bridge & buffer between cloistered and worldly life, which is why they're reduced to dying embers. There needs to be a halfway point in which you can support the organization without full commitment & discipline.
All this is important to keep in mind for future Dark Ages. Hopefully monasteries of the future will preserve the best of modernity in the tumultuous times to come.

replies.title: Re: monastic training and irganization
replies.content:
Very good points. If the Shakers had a 3-tiered system of commitment (like the Manicheans of yore), they might still be going strong today.

Another problem is that American culture is rather hostile to the concept of a quiet, contemplative life.

comments.title: New iOS
comments.content:
This isn't a question as such, but a heads-up or an fyi to the gang. I meant to put it into last month's open post but it happened near the end of that week and I got busy.

Recently our favourite smart /s phone updated their iOS to 18.1.1 which involves a totally new photos app. So I opened it up to look at some pics I had already taken. OMGs it analysis's them.

Usually it's a vague description like 'plant' and asks one to access something to find out what it is, but not always.

It did ID a maple tree in its autumn colours, a lupin spike and a cardinal flower. However it thought that a blue flax plant was a chicory instead.

A picture of a wicker basket (think picnic) full of honey mushrooms I had foraged was identified as 'Food' -would it do the same for destroying angels? That same basket filled with bell peppers I picked before the first frost it thought was 'plant'. It knew a birthday cake close-up was 'food' but the next one with the cake and my 93yo father-in-law was a 'wilted fig'. It fingered my open pollinated broccoli as a 'wild cabbage'.

It did well with a Luna moth and a Polyphemus moth.

Our event-of-the-year was a young Broad-winged Hawk that was (we think) blown from its nest when the remnants of hurricane Deby blew through. I immediately blessed him and gave him protected zone around the house. He had enough wing feathers to glide and to fly up to lower tree branches to spend the night. The rest of the time he hunted insects and the like on the ground. After almost two weeks his tail feathers had grown in and he flew off. About 2 weeks later he came by to say good-bye and show off the new family of Broad-winged Hawks that he would migrate to central or South America with. Anyway the phone said variously (I took a lot of pictures) that he was a Red-shouldered or a Red-tailed Hawk and sometimes a Buteo which is the family of hawks that they all belong to.

So just be aware what photos you take with your phone; big tech is watching!

comments.title: Hindu SOP
comments.content:
In last week’s MM, an anonymous poster asked about a Hindu version of the SOP which JMG could not directly answer. Unfortunately, I read the MM late in the week and so decided to wait until this week’s MM to reply.

I designed and have practiced two different versions of a Hindu SOP. These are variations of the Druid SOP that JMG has published. The first one invoked the traditional Hindu deities of the four directions: after several months of daily practice with very little ‘spark’, I decided to design one that made more sense to me. I found that the second version has a lot of energy, and I have been regularly using it for quite a few years now. I am describing it below in very brief form (practitioners of the SOP should be able to follow), below.

Part 1: in addition to “sky above me”, “earth beneath me”, “fire to my right hand” and “water to my left hand”, I added the names “Hari” (for sky), “Shiva” (for earth), “Shakti” (for fire) and Lakshmi (for water), in a phrase such as “By the sky above me and in the name of Hari”. The wording for the “closing” of Part 1 I have left unchanged.

Part 2: I have made substitutions (animals and gods) as follows:
Yellow gate: “eagle Garuda” (for hawk of May) & “Hari” (for Hu)
Red gate: “great tiger” (for white stag) & “Shakti” (for Sul)
Blue gate: “bull Nandi” (for salmon of wisdom) & “Shiva” (for Hesus)
Green gate: “great elephant” (for great bear) & “Lakshmi” (for Elen)
Orange gate: “Bhoomi” (for Ced)
Purple gate: “Surya” (for Celi)
White gate: “Aum” (for Awen)
I did not change the wording in connection with the animals, because they are still a close fit with Hindu mythology.

Part 3: no changes or substitutions required.

I hope this is of some use to others! For the sake of brevity, I have avoided providing a rationale for each name and animal – but if anyone really wants to know, I am happy to explain (nothing has been randomly chosen).

Ron M (Cosmic Oom name of the week: Maroon Ponderous Lobster)

replies.title: Re: Hindu SOP
replies.content: Thank you very much for this!

replies.title: Re: Hindu SOP
replies.content:
I'm not Hindu, but developed a relationship with a Hindu deity and wanted to incorporate that into SoP practice. After some research, experimentation and plenty of prayer and meditation, I settled on:

East: Hanuman
South: Durga
West: Ganesha
North: Lakshmi
Below: Shiva
Above: Vishnu

For what it's worth, it's been working well enough for me so far.

replies.title: Re: Hindu SOP
replies.content:
Interesting - your associations appear to fit well.
That's one of the many things that I like about the SOP - there is no single 'right way' to do it!

Ron M

comments.content:

The chase scene out of Innsmouth and the descent into the cavern under Red Hook come particularly to mind.

replies.title: Re: The Shoggoth Concerto is special
replies.content:
Thank you -- I really appreciate hearing this.

The Shoggoth Concerto is far and away my favorite of my novels; it embodied the vision, the mood, and the ideas I wanted to express more perfectly than anything else I've written so far. In a very real sense all my novels are guided meditations -- that's what a novel is, after all, a sequence of thoughts and experiences through which the reader's mind moves -- and that one went deeper and accomplished more than my others.

replies.title: Re: The Shoggoth Concerto is special
replies.content:
I'll second this, and add that the Nyogtha Variations (which are my favourite of the two, though both, plus A Voyage to Hyperborea, really sealed the set for me and put the whole framework of the main storyline into a beautiful perspective) was instrumental (literally, and pun intended, and no pun intended, all at once) in reviving my interest in music in general, in the recorder in specific, and in opera and early music which had lain dormant for decades. It re-awakened something in me and for that I'm truly grateful, and then to that awakened part of me it spoke hope, and beauty, and joy, and purpose.

I re-read the whole series at least once a year and when I come to these novels in turn it feels like a special treat. comments.title: Dealers and St. Muerte
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,
Some days ago, the police arrested drug dealers and found a video with a ritual consecrated to Santa Muerte.
I heard you don’t see videos, so I’ll describe it here. In an altar, there are three figures of Santa Muerte surrounded with drug bags, white candles, $20 bills, vases with red and white roses, a dish with fruits, schnapps and skulls.
A man, supposedly Ecuadorian but with somewhat Central American accent, is praying verbosely in Spanish. Some of that prayings are:

“We ask for your blessings, most holy Mother, so that everything we do goes well, so that everything we do will go wonderful and so that the things we do, yes they are dirty, did under the “no protection”… we know that God does not go with the bad, but in your name, merciful mother, we ask you to have mercy on us and to see our hearts. (…)
“Our heart is humble and the things we do, even if they are not right, we do them because of the mercy of God the Father. May each and every one of the girls who are walking with this arrive safely (…) may each and every person who puts their hand on them cannot touch (the drug).
“There is no evil, since evil things are that way because of the hands of men. (…)
“In your merciful name blessed Mother, we consecrate this working in the name of (inaudible), amen.”

I work in Law enforcement and border security. I’d like to protect my nation and keep away criminals and drug dealers. How can I fight this kind of working?
FWIW, I'm in LRM lesson 8.
Thanks,
Edu

replies.title: Re: Dealers and St. Muerte
replies.content: You need to know a lot more magic before you can take on this sort of thing, but you're going about it the right way. Finish LRM and go on from there to Paths of Wisdom (which covers the philosophy and meditations) and Circles of Power (which covers the magical practices). Work through both books. That'll give you the skills you'll need to dissolve their protective spells and ward off whatever hostile magic they may throw at you.

replies.title: Re: Dealers and St. Muerte
replies.content:
Wow. It's sad that they aren't reserving the epithet "most holy Mother" for Mary. The working was already rather dubious, but that's just asking for disarrangement and who knows what kinds of unexpected consequences. I don't suppose you could publish a PSA across the battle lines about that.

(Or maybe there's not as much problem as I think in syncretizing Santa Muerte with Santa Maria? It's not like I can really be confident in predicting that there is a problem.)

Some possible points of ... not so much countermagic as complementary magic ... to keep in mind:

- Of course, even if it would be high-handed and perhaps unethical to push for this yourself, you wouldn't exactly mind any more than the practitioner if the girls who were muling the drugs were in the long run safe in some sense -- just, not the same sense that the practitioner had in mind. (Or, ultimately, that most of the people involved were prosperous or otherwise well-off, in at least some way.)

- You're presumably sworn to uphold the law, so I suppose you're not necessarily in a position to push in the direction of trying to hold the practitioner to their justifying rationalization, by having the illegal drugs only get through to people for whom they would be legitimately medically beneficial or something. (I recently heard a story of someone who had to get black tar heroin on the black market to taper off an opioid prescription, after their prescribing physician abruptly abandoned their practice with no recourse for their patients due to the psychological fallout of a horrific crime that had been committed against one of their children.)

Maybe there's something dangerous about my tendency to think about what things like that ritual might look like from Santa Muerte's perspective and in terms of her commonly-understood priorities, but it's hard not to.

JMG: About four years back, I remember reading here or on the main blog a mention of someone using some exotic Roman religious technique that was like an anti-propitiation targeted for someone else, or an attempt to outbid someone else's offering and appeal to a god to subvert their implied convenant with that person (a very Roman concept, I guess?) -- specifically Neptune and something about the Biden presidential campaign and boats. But the loanword I remember them using to describe this technique had as its English present tense transitive form "onvole", and that's not coming up in a useful way on internet searches. Did whoever wrote the term misspell it? Have I misremembered it?

replies.title: Re: Dealers and St. Muerte
replies.content: Somebody's garbled it. The Latin word is evocatio, the root of the English "evocation." You can find out quite a bit about it online; the search string "evocatio roman gods" brought up pages of them just now.

replies.title: Re: Dealers and St. Muerte
replies.content: Oh, okay, that sounds like it has a lot of extra stuff going on so that it wouldn't be quite the right concept to use as a starting point for how to counter-bid drug traffickers.

replies.title: Re: Dealers and St. Muerte
replies.content: Oh, you could use it for that, but it would take a thorough mastery of the relevant traditions -- and plenty to offer the deity in question, of course.

Not sure if this helps or not, as I haven't seen it exactly connected to magic or occult practice, but Heathens tend to think and talk about ethics in a non-Christian, non-Classical religious context. I haven't done the deep-dive on this I want to yet, but two books that come to mind are We Are Our Deeds by Eric Wodening and Oaths, Shild, Frith, Luck & Wyrd: Five Essays Exploring Heathen Ethical Concepts and their Use Today by Winifred Hodge Rose. The short version from my admittedly shallow-thus-far engagement is a slightly different flavor of virtue ethics than you'll find from the Stoics and other classical sources, but the same basic idea: cultivate excellence across a number of admirable qualities with the goal of an overall strong character.

Also, even more tenuously related, but it came to mind, so I figured I'd share: a book just came out called The Bushido of Bitcoin by Alexander Svetski. Obviously from the title, Svetski is something of a techno-optimist, but the book is about how to cultivate character in a way that supports enacting your will in an ethical way in a dangerous, chaotic world, and it takes spirituality seriously (Svetski is a Christian, I believe) so maybe it's more relevant to magic and the occult than it sounds at first. Also despite the name, it draws at least as heavily on the Christian chivalric tradition as on the Japanese Bushido tradition, and draws comparisons between them where it can. I've only given it a brief skim and read the introduction so far, but I was surprised by the pretty close correspondence between its set of virtues and those I derived for myself a few years back mostly from Stoic and Heathen influences.

As I said, I know these aren't exactly what you were looking for, but hope something in here helps!

Jeff

For anyone whose hackles get raised by references to cryptocurrency: from the context, it sounds like the reference to Bitcoin is largely because of how its design tries to assume as little as possible about anyone's good intentions, and how it creates a sort of "wild west" situation where everyone has to be personally responsible for guarding their authenticating credentials, because the pitiless computation offers no recourse if someone gets at those credentials and transfers all your consensus-bucks to themselves. In a way, Bitcoin tries to expose and stay directly engaged with certain realities and dangers that most modern governments try to paper over in the currency subjective environments that they try to provide their citizens and foreign speculators with, even though it is more "unreal" in a different sense because of how there is no centrally coordinated wealth-bearing institution backing any of its notional value.

I guess, given its dependence on a complex interlocking functional integration between its engineered technological properties and its manipulation of subjective assumptions and perspective-formation about how valuation works, Bitcoin could be said to be a remarkable work of technomagic. I wonder if "manipulate everyone by creating Bitcoin because you have activist goals about governments not being able to inflate their currencies" is an example of the kind of act that Svetski considers hardnosed virtue.

comments.title: Paedrig's Pentacles
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer. I have a question regarding sigils - the shapes and symbols that represent an intention or desire you wish to will into existence. I read a good deal about them when I tinkered with chaos magic. However, I personally find the wide-open loosey-goosey nature of chaos magic to be hard to take seriously, and would prefer a more traditional method, a something with more gravitas, a well-worn track in the aether if you will.

So, I have recently been attempting to manifest certain positive changes in my life. I began by focusing on what I actually want. Everything I read indicates I should create a statement, in the present tense, where I have already acheived my desires. Something akin to, "I live in contentment in my home with my family," for domestic and financial bliss, or "My wife and I make one another very happy," to bring about romantic success. So far, so good?

Then, most chaos magic tells you to just draw something that kind of represents that idea, or use letters from the saying, and just kind of throw it together in whatever way you wish. As I researched further, I discovered older ways of making sigils - using magic squares.

Then it hit me - talisman of Jupiter I crafted with your guidance has a pentacle of jupiter on one side, and a magic square on the other! Did you, perchance, intend to guide me to make a magical artifact I can use to craft sigils?

So, I dove deeper into researching creating sigils with magic squares, and found the following method for doing this using solomonic techniques, which seems appropriate given that I have, you know, the SOLOMONIC second pentacle of Jupiter on the talisman opposite the square and all.

So, the method I have compiled works as follows. You take the statement - say, "I live in contentment in my own house with my family," - and translate it into hebrew. Then, you remove all duplicates of the same letters. Finally, you take the hebrew alphabet and do some quick numerology, changing each letter to its numerical order in the hebrew alphabet - one for aleph, two for bet, three for gimmel, and so on. You then take these numbers and proceed to look at the corresponding locations on the magic square and then sort of... play connect the dots, going from number to number in the same order as the letters of your statement of intention, and in this way create the sigil.

Is this method correct? Do you have any suggestions or corrections? What about charging the sigil - should I try to focus on the intention? Maybe while meditating under the light of Jupiter?

I have a small little obelisk that sits at the center of my ritual circle, and I discovered to my amazement that it fits exactly over the magic square! So... I'm envisioning a small paper square, cut to the exact size of the square on the talisman, with the sigil traced right atop the square, and then set in the exact center of the circle, the obelisk atop it, oreinted to the compass directions, placed directly below me during ritual so energy flows along my central channel pass right through it. Problem is, I'm only really familiar with the sphere of protection. I feel like I should cleanse the energy in the sigil... and then do something else to charge the sigil. No idea what though. Or what to do with the sigil once its charged.

Your guidance is always appreciated!

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content:
Um, let me start with some basics. First, the whole modern sigil magic business was invented by Austin Osman Spare in the mid-20th century. It's not traditional, and I know a lot of people who've had very limited success with it. It's not a technique I recommend.

Second, there are no points given for making things hypercomplex. Mixing together Spareian sigil magic with Hebrew numerology and Chaldean magic squares isn't going to get you any more oomph than you'd get by a much simpler method.

My suggestion? Take one of your two intentions and use it as a good old-fashioned New Thought-style affirmation, following the instructions you'll find here:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/64539.html

Don't tell anyone that you're doing it. Repeat the words until they lose all meaning to your conscious mind and just become sound -- a hundred times a day works well for this. It's a simple, straightforward method and it works very, very well.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content:
No points for making things overcomplicated - ha! I've been accused of doing that a lot. In case yoy can't tell, my mind often goes a mile a minute, and spews forth verbiage and ideas at absurd rates. I've been told my mind and mouth operate on a frequency quite a bit beyond most people's, and yeah, it does annoy people a lot. I maybe could try to focus on reigning that in a bit. Any suggested meditations for an overactive mind that need to learn to keep things simpler?

I'll use the affirmations as you suggest. Thanks again for guiding me out of the elaborate maze my mind had constructed.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content: What worked for me -- as I tend to the same kind of mental hyperdrive -- was repeated collisions with the real world, which tends to make short shrift of all those complexities and respond better to simple, straightforward approaches. There may be an alternative to ramming one's head against a succession of brick walls, but I don't know of one. ;-)

comments.title: Musicality?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Maxine here. I sing in a community choir and enjoy it very much. That said, I know my voice is not very good. I am a tenor and do not have great voice control. Is there a way I could help myself have a good singing voice in my next incarnation? I am devoted to Apollo so would prayer to Apollo be a good option? What are some other options?

I have also decided I would like to be born, next incarnation, into a musical family. The kind of family that likes to play music together and sing together. They don't have to be performers. I just want a nice, calm family that enjoys making music. Is there any way I can help this to happen?

My cello studies are going well. I have a new teacher as my first teacher moved to an island a couple of hours by car south of us. My new teacher is fabulous and holds a Masters in Music from Seoul, Korea. She is frighteningly powerful and intelligent but also kind and very generous. I love her devotedly after only a few months with her.

She asked me if I thought I was improving. I told her is is hard to see if a glacier is melting, especially when you are the glacier. She told me I was improving and made a motion with her hand as of an aircraft taking off. That is nice to know but I am still in the first foothills of India on my way to climb the Himalayas.

Thank you for all your teachings. I continue to make improvements and am on chapter 36 of your Occult Philosophy Workbook. That too is doing me good.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Musicality?
replies.content: Prayers to Apollo would certainly be sensible, and there's also the simple fact that working on a skill in one life means that you'll have a talent for it the next time around. That's how Mozart became Mozart, you know -- many lives devoted to music. (I'm tending more and more to the belief that he was Antonio Vivaldi in his last pre-Mozart life.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: On the last of the potlucks we talked about martian astrology. One of the things we talked about was that then mars and the earth would switch places. What influence would the earth have on a martian in its horoscope and theoretical society? The one of Gaia somehow I would assume.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's exactly what we don't know. Astrology is an empirical science, not a theoretical one: the influences of the planets were determined, not on an a priori basis, but by recording what the planets did and comparing that to political and dynastic events on Earth. (Some of the clay tablet records that went into that process have survived.) So Martian astrologers would have to spend quite a bit of time comparing the place of Earth in the zodiacal signs, its aspects with the other planets, and the effects of its retrograde periods to Martian events to figure out what it would mean to them.
